question: $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ represents a/an
explanation: In conjugated system, double bonds are present at alternate positions, in cumulative system, double bonds are present at adjacent positions while in isolated system, there is a large difference, between the positions of double bond.Hence, $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ represents an isolated system.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Stabilities of Conjugated Dienes, Alkenes and Alkynes

question:
Assertion: All carbon atoms in 1,3-butadiene are $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$ hybridised.


Reason: 1,3-Butadiene is a conjugated diene.
explanation: In 1,3-butadiene, $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}\right)$ all the carbon atoms are having a pi bond and three sigma bonds. So all carbon atoms are $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$ hybridised. 1,3 Butadiene has alternate double bonds. So, it is called conjugate diene. Thus, option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Stabilities of Conjugated Dienes, Alkenes and Alkynes

question:
The alkane which is next to methane in homologous series can be prepared from which of the following reactions?
I. $2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Br} \xrightarrow[\text { dry ether }]{\mathrm{Na}}$
II. $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{CaO}, \Delta]{\mathrm{NaOH}}$
III. $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}_{2} / \mathrm{Pt}}$
IV. $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Br} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{H}^{+}]{\mathrm{Zn}}$
Correct answer is
explanation:
Homologous series of alkane has the formula $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{H}_{2 \mathrm{n}+2}$
Next to methane homologous series molecule is Ethane, $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}$.
I. Wurtz Reaction:
$2{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{Br}}\xrightarrow[{{\text{ dry ether }}}]{{{\text{Na}}}}\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}}\limits_{{\text{Ethane}}} $
II. Decarboxylation:
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{CaO}, \Delta]{\mathrm{NaOH}} \mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
III. ${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{CH}} = {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}\xrightarrow{{{{\text{H}}_2}/{\text{pt}}}}\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}}\limits_{{\text{Propane}}} $
IV. $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Br} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{H}^{+}]{\mathrm{Zn}} \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$
So only I and IV give ethane.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Which of the following reaction produce alkane as the product?
I. $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{COO} \stackrel{\ominus}{\mathrm{O}} \stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{N}} \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{NaOH} \xrightarrow[\Delta]{\mathrm{CaO}}$
II. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955583/preptracker/questions/q_105146_question_1764955583425.png
III. $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{CCH}_{3} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}}$
IV. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955584/preptracker/questions/q_105146_question_1764955584499.png
explanation:
(I) $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{C} \stackrel{-}{\mathrm{O}} \mathrm{O} \stackrel{+}{\mathrm{Na}}+\mathrm{NaOH} \xrightarrow[\Delta]{\mathrm{CaO}} \underset{\text { (Alkane) }}{(\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{H})}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$
The mixture of $\mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{CaO}$ is called sodalime when it reacts with sodium salt of carboxylic acid, it gives corresponding alkane.
(II) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955586/preptracker/questions/q_105146_explanation_1764955586078.png
The above reaction is called Clemmensen reduction.
(III) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C} \cdot \mathrm{CH}_{3} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}}$ No reaction

question: An alkane with molecular mass 72 formed only one substitution product. The number of $1^{\circ}$ hydrogens in it is.
explanation:
Let the molecular formula of the alkane be $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{H}_{2 \mathrm{n}+2}$. Molecular mass of the alkane $=\mathrm{nC}+(2 \mathrm{n}+2) \mathrm{H}$
$ = {\rm{n}} \times 12 + (2{\rm{n}} + 2) \times 1$
$ = 14\,{\rm{n}} + 2$
$14\,{\rm{n}} + 2 = 72$
$14\,{\rm{n}} = 72 - 2 = 70$
${\rm{n}} = \frac{{70}}{{14}} = 5$
Thus, the molecular formula of the alkane is $\mathrm{C}_{5} \mathrm{H}_{12}$. It can have three isomers.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764948691/preptracker/questions/q_22179_explanation_1764948691082.png
It is neopentane which forms one substitution product as it has only one type of hydrogen. Number of $1^{\circ}$ hydrogens $=12$.

question:
How many of the following compounds will have lower boiling points as compared to n-pentane?
2,2-Dimethylpropane, 2-methylpropane.
2-methylbutane, 2,2-dimethylbutane,
2-methylpentane, 2-methylbut-2-ene,
pent-2-yne
explanation:
Factors that affect boiling point of alkanes are:
i. Number of C-atoms: As the no. of C-atom increases, the boiling point increases.
ii. Branching: The boiling point decreases with increase in branching in isomeric alkanes.
Thus, 2,2-dimethylpropane (282.5 K) ,
2-methylbutane ( 301 K ) and 2-methylpropane
( 261.3 K ) will have lower boiling point as compared to n-pentane ( 309.1 K ).
Also, boiling points of alkenes and alkynes are higher as compared to corresponding alkanes.

question:
Assertion: Staggered conformation of ethane is $1.25 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ more stable than the eclipsed conformation.

Reason: The two conformations of ethane cannot be separated at room temperature.
explanation:
Staggered conformation is more stable because any two hydrogen atoms on adjacent carbon atoms are as far apart as possible. Also the repulsion between the electron clouds of $\sigma$ - bonds of two non-bonded hydrogen atoms is minimum. The energy difference of
$12.55 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ is not large enough to prevent rotation at room temperature. As a result it is not possible to separate the two conformations of ethane. So, option (2) is correct .

question:
Assertion: The octane number of branched chain hydrocarbons is higher than that of unbranched chain hydrocarbons.


Reason: The branched chain hydrocarbons are more volatile than unbranched chain hydrocarbons.
explanation: Octane no. increases with branching. Branched alkanes have a number of tertiary carbon-hydrogen bonds. Such hydrogens are easily abstracted in the combustion chain reactions, and speed up the propagation of the explosive events that take place in the piston chamber. Straight chain "normal" alkanes contain only primary and secondary groups which are given out slowly. Straight chains have higher boiling points due to large surface area. So, option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Dipole moment of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Cl}$ is more than $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{~F}$.


Reason: $\mathrm{F}$ is more electronegative than $\mathrm{Cl}$.
explanation:
Dipole moment $=\delta \times \mathrm{d}$;
The $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond is longer than $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{F}$ bond $(\mathrm{Cl}$ being larger in size than F). The large value of ' ${\text{d}}$ ' in $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Cl}$ predominates over $\delta $ value to give
higher value of $\mu $ for $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Cl} . \mathrm{F}$ is more electronegative than ' $\mathrm{Cl}$ '. But it is not the correct explanation. So, option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Iodination of alkanes is made in presence of $\mathrm{HgO}$ or $\mathrm{HIO}_{3}$.

Reason: Iodination is very slow and reversible process.
explanation:
During ionisation of alkanes, one of the products (HI) acts as strong reducing agent to reverse the reaction.
$\mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{I}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{I}+\mathrm{I}_{2}$
Therefore, iodination is made in presence of $\mathrm{HgO}$ or $\mathrm{HIO}_{3}$ which utilises $\mathrm{HI}$ formed in the reaction and give better yield of iodoalkane.
$\mathrm{HIO}_{3}+5 \mathrm{HI} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{I}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
So, option (1) is correct.

explanation: Wurtz reaction involves the formation of alkanes by treating alkyl halide with sodium in ether. Two alkyl radicals join together to form an alkane. question:

Reason: $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bond is polar in nature.
explanation:
The bond energy of C-C (i.e., ${\text{(s}}{{\text{p}}^{\text{3}}}{\text{ - s}}{{\text{p}}^{\text{3}}}{\text{)}}$ is
$83\,\,{\text{kcal}}\,\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^ - }$ and bond energy of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$
(i.e., $\mathrm{sp}^{3}-\mathrm{s}$ ) is $99\,\,{\text{kcal}}\,\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^ - }$. Thus, fission of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}$ bond takes place easily in comparison to $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bond in alkanes. The electronegativity difference between carbon and hydrogen is small and thus $\mathrm{C}$ - $\mathrm{H}$ bond is almost non-polar. So, option (3) is correct.

question:
$\mathrm{RMgBr}+\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CHOH} \rightarrow$ hydrocarbon $(\mathrm{A})$
'A' occupies 1.4 L per gm at NTP, hence RMgBr is
explanation:
$\mathrm{RMgBr}+\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{OH} \rightarrow \mathrm{RH}$
Given: 1.4 lit gas (Hydrocarbon) $=1 \mathrm{gm}$ at NTP
$\therefore 22.4$ lit gas $=\dfrac{1}{1.4} \times 22.4 \Rightarrow 16 \mathrm{gm}$
Molecular mass of hydrocarbon $=16 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mole}$
$\therefore$ Hydrocarbon is $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$
subject: Chemistry

question: $2.84 \mathrm{~g}$ of methyl iodide was completely converted into methyl magnesium iodide and was decomposed by excess of ethanol. The volume of the gaseous hydrocarbon produced at NTP will be:
explanation:
1 mole $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{I}$ gives 1 mole $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{MgI}$ and one mole $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{MgI}$ gives one mole $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{I}+\mathrm{Mg} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{MgI}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{MgI}+\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}(1 \mathrm{~mole}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OMgl}$
Given moles of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{I}=\dfrac{2.84}{142}=0.02 \mathrm{moles}$
It means 0.02 mole $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ is formed.
$\therefore$ vol.of $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ at $\mathrm{STP}=0.02 \times 22.4 \mathrm{~L}=0.448 \mathrm{~L}$

question: One gram of an aliphatic bromide was completely reacted with $\mathrm{Mg}$ and then treated with water, ${\text{182}}\,\,{\text{ml}}$  at STP, of a gas evolved in the process. The gas was found to be stable to $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$. The hydrocarbon is
explanation:
No. of moles of gas evolved $=\dfrac{182}{22400}$
No. of moles of bromide $=\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{M}}$
$\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{M}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{182}}}}{{{\text{22400}}}}\quad {\text{M = 123}}$
Molecular mass of the hydrocarbon $=123-80$ $=43$
Where 80 is the atomic mass of the bromine radical
Molecular mass of the hydrocarbon $=44$
The hydrocarbon therefore is $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{8}$ i.e., propane.

explanation:
All the four options given are monohydric alcohols. When a monohydric alcohol is treated with $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{MgBr}$, the number of moles of $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ gas produced is a equal to the number of moles of alcohol https://image.cleverb.in/upload/2023/09/09/13_(18).png If $\mathrm{M}$ is the molecular weight of alcohol, then
$\dfrac{4.50}{\mathrm{M}}=\dfrac{1.68}{22.4}$
On solving, ${\text{M = 60}}$. Therefore, the unknown alcohol is Propan-1-ol.

question:
Which of the following will give three monobromo derivatives?

(B) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right) \mathrm{CH}_{3}$
(C) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{C}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}$
(D) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right) \mathrm{CH}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right) \mathrm{CH}_{3}$
explanation:
${{\text{1}}^{{\text{st}}}}{\text{ and }}{{\text{6}}^{{\text{th}}}}$ carbon will give same substitute.
${{\text{2}}^{{\text{nd}}}}{\text{ and }}{{\text{5}}^{{\text{th}}}}$ carbon will give same substitute.
${{\text{3}}^{{\text{ rd }}}}{\text{and }}{{\text{4}}^{{\text{ th}}}}$ carbon will give same substitute.
$1+1+1=3$ https://image.cleverb.in/upload/2023/09/09/13_(23).png $1^{\circ}$ carbon attached with $2^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ will give one derivative. Three $1^{\circ}$ carbons attached with $4^{\circ}$ carbon will give same one derivative $\&$ one derivative will be from $2^{\circ}$ carbon.
$1+1+1=3($ total $)$

question:
When 2-methylbutane is chlorinated, the percentage of $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2}-\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}$ is ____
Assume reactivity ratio of
$3^\circ {\text{H}}:2^\circ {\text{H}}:1^\circ {\text{H}} = 5:3.8:1$
explanation:
Chlorination of 2-methylbutane gives 4 monochlorinated products : https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764948079/preptracker/questions/q_20418_explanation_1764948079166.png The reactivity ratio of $3^{\circ} \mathrm{H}: 2^{\circ} \mathrm{H}: 1^{\circ} \mathrm{H}$ towards chlorination is $5: 3.8: 1$
Compound reactivity $\times$ Probability $=$ Number of parts(1) $1 \times 6=6$(2) $5 \times 1=5$(3) $3.8 \times 2=7.6$(4) $1 \times 3=3$
Total number of parts $=21.6$
Percentage of $\mathrm{D}=\dfrac{3}{21.6} \times 100=14 \%$.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Which of the following statements are correct regarding Kolbe's electrolytic process?
I. Alkanes containing even number of C-atoms are prepared.
II. Sodium or potassium salts of carboxylic acids are taken as substrate.
III. Methane can be prepared by this method.
IV. $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas is liberated at anode.
Choose the correct statement.
explanation:
Statements I and II are correct, while the statements III and IV are incorrect
Corrected form are as follows:
III. Methane cannot be prapared by Kolbe's electrolytic process.
IV. $\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ gas is liberated at cathode.
Kolbe's electrolytic reaction:
${\text{RCOONa}}\xrightarrow{{{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}}}\mathop {{\text{R - R + C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}\limits_{{\text{At}}\,\,{\text{Anode}}} {\text{ + }}\mathop {{\text{NaOH + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}}\limits_{{\text{At}}\,\,{\text{Cathode}}} $
So, the option (1) is correct.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Arrange the correct sequence for mechanism of chlorination of methane
I. $\mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{Cl}^{\cdot} \stackrel{\text { hv }}{\longrightarrow} \dot{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_{3}+\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{Cl}$
II. $\mathrm{Cl}-\mathrm{Cl} \stackrel{\text { hv }}{\longrightarrow} 2 \dot{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{l}$
III. $\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^{\text{.}} {{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ + C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}} \to {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ - Cl + C}}{{\text{l}}^{\text{.}}}$
IV. $\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^{\text{.}} {\text{1 + }}\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^{\text{.}} {\text{l }} \to {\text{Cl - Cl}}$
$\dot{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_{3}+\dot{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$
Choose the correct option.
explanation:
Halogenation (chlorination here) of alkane proceeds through free radical mechanism as follows:
II. Initiation Homolysis of chlorine molecule
${\text{Cl}} - {\text{Cl}} \to \mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}^ \bullet }}\limits_{} + \mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}^{^ \bullet }}}\limits_{{\text{ Chlorine free radicals }}} $
I. Propagation ${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_4} + {\text{C}}{{\text{l}}^ \bullet }\xrightarrow{{{\text{ hv }}}}\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^ \bullet {{\text{H}}_3} + {\text{HCl}}$
III. $\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^ \bullet {{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ + Cl - Cl}} \to {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{Cl + }}\mathop {{\text{ C}}}\limits^ \bullet {\text{l}}$
${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{Cl}} + {\text{C}}{{\text{l}}^ \bullet } \to \mathop {\text{C}}\limits^ \bullet {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{Cl}} + {\text{HCl}}$
$\mathop {{\text{ C}}}\limits^ \bullet {{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{Cl + Cl - Cl}} \to {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + }}\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^ \bullet {\text{l }}$
IV. Termination: $\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^ \bullet {\text{l + }}\mathop {{\text{ C}}}\limits^ \bullet {\text{l}} \to {\text{Cl - Cl}}$
${{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^ \bullet {\text{ + }}\mathop {{\text{ C}}}\limits^ \bullet {{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}} \to {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ - C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}$
$\mathop {{\text{ C}}}\limits^ \bullet {{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ + }}\mathop {{\text{ C}}}\limits^ \bullet {\text{l}} \to {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ - Cl}}$
subject: Chemistry

When a mixture of n-butyl bromide and ethyl bromide is treated with sodium metal in the presence of dry ether, then ethane cannot be formed because reaction follows Wurtz-Fittig reaction. It is a type of coupling reaciton.
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Br}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Br}+2 \mathrm{Na} \underset{\text { Ether }}{\stackrel{\text { Dry }}{\longrightarrow}}$
$\mathop {{{\text{C}}_4}{{\text{H}}_9} - {{\text{C}}_4}{{\text{H}}_9}}\limits_{{\text{Octane}}} + \mathop {{{\text{C}}_4}{{\text{H}}_9} - {{\text{C}}_2}{{\text{H}}_5}}\limits_{{\text{ Hexane }}} + \mathop {{{\text{C}}_2}{{\text{H}}_5} - {{\text{C}}_2}{{\text{H}}_5}}\limits_{{\text{ Butane }}} $
subject: Chemistry

question: How many gram of sodium ${\text{(atomic}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{mass 23 u)}}$  is required to prepare one mole of ethane from methyl chloride by Wurtz reaction?
explanation:
The Wurtz reaction between methyl chloride and sodium metal in presence of dry ehter can be written as:
${\text{2C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{Cl + 2Na}}\xrightarrow{{{\text{Dry}}\,\,{\text{ether}}}}\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ - C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}}\limits_{{\text{Ethane}}} {\text{ + 2NaCl}}$
$\Rightarrow 2$ moles of sodium metal reacts to give 1 mole of ethane.
Also,
1 mole of Na metal $=23 \mathrm{~g}$
$\therefore 2$ moles of Na metal $=23 \times 2=46 \mathrm{~g}$
Thus, $46 \mathrm{~g}$ of sodium is required to prepare one mole of ethane from methyl chloride by Wurtz reaction.
subject: Chemistry

question: For the preparation of alkanes, aqueous solution of sodium or potassium salt of carboxylic acid is subjected to
explanation:
Electrolysis of aqueous solutions of sodium or potassium salts of fatty acids gives alkane having twice the number of carbon atoms present in the alkyl group of the acid. This process is kown as Kolbe's electrolytic reaction.
$2 \mathrm{RCOONa} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{RCOO}^{-}+2 \mathrm{Na}^{+}$
At anode: $2 \mathrm{RCOO}^{-}-2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{RCOO}$
$\underset{\text { Unstable }}{2 \mathrm{RCOO}^{-}} \rightarrow \underset{\text { Alkane }}{\mathrm{R}}-\mathrm{R}+2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}$
subject: Chemistry

question: With which halogen the reactions of alkanes are explosive?
explanation:
Reaction of alkanes with fluorine are explosive. This is an explosive process which is difficult to control, so carried out in dark. In this process, replacement of one or more hydrogen of alkane by halogen takes place. For this reaction the order of reactivity of halogen is
$\mathrm{I}_{2} < \mathrm{Br}_{2} < \mathrm{Cl}_{2} < \mathrm{F}_{2}$.

question: Which one of the following will show geometrical isomerism?
explanation:
Conditions to show geometrical isomerism
(1) Restricted rotation around the atoms, e.g., ${\mathrm{\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{N}=\mathrm{N}}}$ and alicyclic compounds.
(2) Same group should not present on the same side of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}}}$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957317/preptracker/questions/q_22408_explanation_1764957316735.png Geometrical isomers do not exist because two H (same group) are present on same carbon. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957318/preptracker/questions/q_22408_explanation_1764957317559.png Geometrical isomers exist as different groups are present on both carbon atoms. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957318/preptracker/questions/q_22408_explanation_1764957318253.png Geometrical isomers do not exist as carbon of the ring has same group of atoms.
So, the correct option is (3).

question:
When subjected to acid catalysed hydration, the order of reactivity of the alkenes is;
I. $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$,
II. $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ and
III. $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ is
explanation:
Acid catalysed hydration of alkenes involve formation of carbocation intermediate. Higher
the stability of carbocation intermediate, more
is the reactivity towards acid
catalysed hydration.
The order of stability of carbocation (formed) is
${\left( {{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}} \right)_{\rm{2}}}\mathop {{\rm{CC}}}\limits^{\rm{ + }} {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}} > {\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}\mathop {{\rm{CH}}}\limits^{\rm{ + }} {\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}} > \mathop {\rm{C}}\limits^{\rm{ + }} {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}} - {\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}$
Thus, the order of reactivity towards acid catalysed hydration is
$\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {{{{\text{(C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{)}}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{C}}\,{\text{ = }}\,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}\,{\text{ > }}\,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{CH}}\,{\text{ = }}\,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}} \\ {\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{I}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, {\text{II}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} } \\ {\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{ > C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ = C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}} \\ {\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{III}}} \\ {{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} } \end{array}$

question:
Assertion: Trans-Pent-2-ene is polar but transbut-2-ene is non-polar.

Reason: The polarity of cis-isomer is more than trans-isomer, which is either non-polar or less polar.
explanation:
In trans-pent-2-ene, the vector sum of all polar bonds does not cancel out to zero due to unsymmetrical distribution of polar bonds. Thus there is a net dipole moment, making the molecule polar.
On the other hand, in trans-but-2-ene, the vector sum of polar bonds is zero due to the symmetrical arrangement of polar bonds that cancel each other out. This results in a nonpolar molecule with no net dipole moment. In general cis alkenes is more polar than trans alkenes but it's not the correct explanation. So, option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

explanation: The stability of a compound increases with the number of hyperconjugating structures it possesses. For example, 2,3-dimethyl-but-2-ene can have twelve hyperconjugation structures, whereas but-2-ene has only six. Assertion is correct but reason is incorrect. Hence, option (3) is correct.

question:
Assertion: 2-Butanol on heating with $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ gives 1-butene and 2-butene.

Reason: Dehydration of 2-butanol follows Saytzeff rule.
explanation:
In the dehydration of secondary and tertiary alcohols, when there is a possibility of formation of two isomers, the hydrogen atom is preferentially eliminated from the adjacent carbon atom with the fewer number of hydrogen atoms. This is known as Saytzeff's rule https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953787/preptracker/questions/q_21286_explanation_1764953787292.png $\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{CH = C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}}\limits_{{\text{1 - Butene(20 % )}}} {\text{ + }}\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{CH = CHC}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}}\limits_{{\text{2 - Butene(80 % )}}} $
So, option (1) is correct

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Propene reacts with $\mathrm{HBr}$ to give isopropyl bromide.

Reason: Addition of hydrogen halide to alkenes follows Markownikoff's rule.
explanation: Addition of unsymmetrical reagent to unsymmetrical alkene takes place according to Markownikoff's rule, i.e., negative part of the addendum goes to the carbon atom with less number of hydrogen atoms.  https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953786/preptracker/questions/q_21268_explanation_1764953785967.png  So, option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: But-2-ene shows geometrical isomerism but but-1-ene does not show.

Reason: Geometrical isomerism arises due to the restricted rotation of the double bond.
explanation: In but-1-ene, the carbon atom linked by double bond is attached with two hydrogen atoms (similar groups) and thus, it does not show geometrical isomerism. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953785/preptracker/questions/q_21265_explanation_1764953785126.png   So, option (2) is correct.

Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: Alkenes are reactive than alkanes.


Reason: Alkenes possess $\pi$ bonds.
explanation: Alkanes have only sigma bonds, but in alkenes, there is a a $\pi$ bond. Pi-bond is weaker than a $\sigma$ bond and is easily broken. The $\pi$-electrons are less firmly bound to carbon nuclei and are thus reactive. So, option (1) is correct

question: Correct order for reaction with alcoholic $\mathrm{KOH}$.
explanation:
When alcoholic $\mathrm{KOH}$ reacts with haloakanes, the reaction (rate) depends on the following factor:
(I) Size of halogen
(II) Degree of C - atom, to whcih the halogen atom is bonded, i.e. more the degree of haloalkane, more is its reactivity.
As, bromine atom is large in size, thus https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957295/preptracker/questions/q_20943_explanation_1764957294289.png Hence, correct order is A > B > C > D
Thus, option (2) is correct.

question: Formation of alcohol by oxymercuration demercuration of alkenes
explanation:
Oxymercuration-demercuration takes place in the following steps:
(I) Generation of an electrophile (mercurinium ion)
${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - {\text{COO}} - {\text{Hg}} - {\text{OCOC}}{{\text{H}}_3}$
$ \to \mathop {\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{COO}}}\limits_{{\text{ (electrophile) }}} - {\text{H}}{{\text{g}}^ + } + {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{CO}}{{\text{O}}^ - }}\limits_{} $
(II) Attack of electrophile on double bond of alkene https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953722/preptracker/questions/q_20929_explanation_1764953721731.jpg (III) Addition of water https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953722/preptracker/questions/q_20929_explanation_1764953722430.png (IV) Reduction of ${\text{C - HgOCOC}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}$ bond https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953723/preptracker/questions/q_20929_explanation_1764953723254.jpg Hence, oxymercuration-demercuration process involves reduction and bridged mercurinium ion formation.

options.text: Does not involve rearrangement and carbocation.

question: An unsaturated hydrocarbon ' ${\text{(A)}}$ ' adds to two molecules of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ and on reductive ozonolysis gives Butane-1, 4-dial, Ethanal and Propanone. Give the structure of ' ${\text{(A)}}$ ', with its IUPAC name.
explanation:
Two molecules of hydrogen add on ' ${\text{A}}$ ' this shows that ' $\mathrm{A}$ ' is either an alkadiene or an alkyne.
On reductive ozonolysis ' ${\text{A}}$ ' gives three fragments, one of which is dialdehyde. Hence, the molecule has broken down at two sites. Therefore, 'A' has two double bonds.
It gives the following three fragments:
$\mathrm{OHC}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CHO}, \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CO}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$
Hence, its structure as deduced from the three fragments must be https://image.cleverb.in/upload/2023/09/09/13_(17).png https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764948385/preptracker/questions/q_20783_explanation_1764948385443.png

question: The reactivity of the given alkenes towards hydrogen is
explanation: The reactivity of alkenes towards hydrogen depends on the hyperconjugation effect in the alkene. More the hyperconjugation effect, more is the stability of alkene and lesser is the reactivity. The hyperconjugation effect will depend on the number of alpha hydrogen atoms. "A" has three alpha hydrogen atoms, "B" has six and "C" has nine. The hyperconjugation effect in the given alkene is $\mathrm{B}>\mathrm{A}>\mathrm{C}$. Thus, stability is $\mathrm{B}>$ $\mathrm{A}>\mathrm{C}$ and hence reactivity of alkene with $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ is in the order $\mathrm{C}>\mathrm{A}>$ $\mathrm{B}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Addition Reactions of Alkenes: Hydrogenation, Halogenation

question: In the presence of peroxide, $\mathrm{HCl}$ and $\mathrm{HI}$ do not give anti-Markovnikoff's addition of alkenes because
explanation: Anti-Markovnikov's addition is possible only in case of $\mathrm{HBr}$ and not in $\mathrm{HCl}$ and $\mathrm{HI}$. In $\mathrm{HBr}$ both the chain initiation and propagation steps are exothermic, while in $\mathrm{HCl}$, first step is exothermic, and second step is endothermic and in $\mathrm{HI}$, no step is exothermic. Hence $\mathrm{HCl}$ and $\mathrm{HI}$ do not undergo anti-Markovnikov's addition.

options.text: One of the steps is endothermic in $\mathrm{HCl}$ and $\mathrm{HI}$
$\mathrm{HCl}$ is oxidising and the $\mathrm{HI}$ is reducing
All the steps are exothermic is $\mathrm{HCl}$ and $\mathrm{HI}$

question: Identify the following compound which exhibits geometrical isomerism
explanation:
The necessary and sufficient condition for a compound to exhibit geometrical isomerism is that the two groups attached to the same double bonded carbon must be different, i.e., alkenes of the following types show cis-trans isomerism. $\mathrm{abC}=\mathrm{Cab}, \mathrm{abC}=\mathrm{Ccd}, \mathrm{abC}=\mathrm{Cax}, \mathrm{abC}=\mathrm{Cbx}$
Thus, out of the given examples, only but-2-ene exhibits geometrical isomerism. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959239/preptracker/questions/q_19732_explanation_1764959238508.png

question:
Arrange the following in the correct order of their acidic strength.
I. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$
II. $\mathrm{CH} \equiv \mathrm{CH}$
III. $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{CH}$
IV. $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$
explanation:
The acidic strength can be determined on the basis of hybridisation. More is the s - character higher will be the acidity. Triple bond has highest s-character while double bond has a less s-character than triple bond but higher than a single bond
${{\text{H}}_2}{\text{C}}\mathop {\mathop = \limits_{{\text{s}}{{\text{p}}^2}} }\limits^{\text{I}} {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}\quad \quad \quad \mathop {\mathop {{\text{CH}} \equiv {\text{CH}}}\limits^{{\text{II}}} }\limits_{{\text{sp}}} $
$\mathop {\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - {\text{C}} \equiv {\text{CH}}}\limits^{{\text{III}}} }\limits_{{\text{sp}}} \quad \quad {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - \mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^{{\text{IV}}} {{\text{H}}_3}}\limits_{{\text{s}}{{\text{p}}^{\text{3}}}} $
So, correct order of acidic strength is
IV $ < $ I $ < $ III $ < $ II

(II) $\mathrm{F}_{3} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{CH}$

(IV) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CHCH}_{3}$
explanation:
Greater the electronegative atom attached to the carbon chain, greater will be its acidic nature, i.e. $\mathrm{F}_{3} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{CH}$ is the most acidic compound.
Alkyl group ( $-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ ) decreases the acidic nature hence, lesser the alkyl group in carbon chain, greater will be its acidic nature. i.e. $\underset{\text { Less }-\mathrm{CH}_{3}}{\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{CH}>} \underset{\text { two }-\mathrm{CH}_{3} \text { groups }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}}$
Order of acidic nature depends on follows the order : alkyne > alkene > alkane.
Hence, order of acidity is $\mathop {{{\text{F}}_3}{\text{C}} - {\text{C}} \equiv {\text{CH}}}\limits_{{\text{More}}\,\,{\text{electronegative}}\,\,\left( {{\text{II}}} \right)} $ $ > \mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - {\text{C}} \equiv {\text{CH}}}\limits_{{\text{Only}}\,\,{\text{one}} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}\,({\text{I}})} > \mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{C}} \equiv {\text{CC}}{{\text{H}}_3}}\limits_{{\text{two}}\,\,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}\,\,{\text{groups}}\,{\text{(III)}}} > $ ${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{CH}}\mathop = \limits_{({\text{IV}})} {\text{CHC}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}$
i.e. II $>$ I $>$ III $>$ IV.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Acidic Character of Alkynes and Conversion of Lower to Higher Alkynes

${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}} - {\rm{C}} \equiv \overline {\rm{C}} \mathop {{\rm{Na}}}\limits^{\rm{ + }} {\rm{ + }}\mathop {{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{CHC}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}}\limits_{{\rm{(B)}}} - {\rm{Br}} \to $
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_3} - {\rm{C}} \equiv {{\rm{C}}_{({\rm{C}})}} - {\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_2}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_2}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_3} + {\rm{NaBr}}$

question: The ascending acidity order of the following ${\mathrm{H}}$ atoms is
explanation:
Acidity ${\mathrm{\propto-I}}$ effect ${\mathrm{\propto s \%}}$ character
${\mathrm{\propto \dfrac{1}{+ \text { Ieffect }}}}$
In https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764948766/preptracker/questions/q_22432_explanation_1764948765824.png is attached to ${\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{sp}}}}$.

${\mathrm{\%}}$ of s character : ${\mathrm{\mathrm{sp}>\mathrm{sp}^{2}>\mathrm{sp}^{3}}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{3}}}$ shows +I effect. In ${\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{C}^{-}, 3 \mathrm{CH}_{3}}}$ groups are attached, which destabilise the ${\mathrm{3^{\circ}}}$ carbanion. Whereas in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}^{-}}}$, only one ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{3}}}$ group is attached, so ascending acidity order of H atoms is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764948769/preptracker/questions/q_22432_explanation_1764948768838.png https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764948770/preptracker/questions/q_22432_explanation_1764948770315.png Therefore, the correct order will be (III) < (IV) ${\mathrm{ < }}$ (II) ${\mathrm{ < }}$ (I). So, the correct option is (1).

explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764948467/preptracker/questions/q_21464_explanation_1764948467459.png
cis-2-butene has lower melting point than trans2-butene as trans-form has compactely packed crystal lattice.

question:
Which of the following order is correct regarding acidic character of hydrocarbons given below?
I. $\mathrm{CH} \equiv \mathrm{CH}>\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}>\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$
II. $\mathrm{HC} \equiv \mathrm{CH}>\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{CH}>\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{CCH}_{3}$
explanation:
Both the orders regarding acidity of hydrocarbon are correct.
I. Acidic strength $\propto$ percent ${\text{s - character [s - character }}$ is $50 \%$ in alkynes, $33 \%$ in alkanes and $25 \%$ in alkanes].
II. Inductive effect defines acidic strength and polarity of C-H bond of alkyne. Groups having + I-effect increases electron density around $\mathrm{C-}$ $\mathrm{H}$ bond, hence polarity decreases and acidic strength of alkyne decreases.
So, the option (3) is correct.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Acidic Character of Alkynes and Conversion of Lower to Higher Alkynes

question:
Which of the following are not aromatic?
(A) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951913/preptracker/questions/q_105187_question_1764951913006.png
(B) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951914/preptracker/questions/q_105187_question_1764951913798.png
(C) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951914/preptracker/questions/q_105187_question_1764951914502.png
(D) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951915/preptracker/questions/q_105187_question_1764951915222.png
(E) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951916/preptracker/questions/q_105187_question_1764951916263.png
(F) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951917/preptracker/questions/q_105187_question_1764951916995.png
explanation:
Conditions for species to be an aromatic are:
(i) The aromatic structures should follow
Huckel's rule, i.e. species have $(4 n+2) \pi$ electrons in its structure where $\mathrm{n}=0,1,2,3 \ldots$ so on
(ii) Should be planar ring structure.
(A) It follows Huckel's rule and has planar structure thus, is an aromatic ($\because \mathrm{n}=1, \pi \mathrm{e}^{-}=6$).
(B) It does not follow Huckel's rule ( $\because$ has $8 \pi$ electrons with $\mathrm{n}=2$).
(C) It follows Huckel's rule and has planar structure. Thus, is an aromatic ($\because \mathrm{n}=1, \pi \mathrm{e}^{-}=6$).
(D) It follows Huckel's rule and has planar structure as it has $n=3$ and $14 \pi$ electrons.
(E) It does not follow Huckel's rule. Thus is not an aromatic compound ($\mathrm{n}=1, \pi \mathrm{e}^{-}=4$).
(F) It also does not follow Huckel's rule. Thus, is not an aromatic ($\mathrm{n}=0, \pi \mathrm{e}^{-}=4$)
Hence, species (B), (E) and (F) are not aromatic and option (2) is the correct answer.

question: The order of stability of aromatic hydrocarbons given below is
explanation:
Stability of aromatic compound is directly proportional to resonance energy per ring. Benzene has resonance energy of $36\,{\text{kcal}}/{\text{mol}},$ per ring, naphthalene has $30.3\,{\text{kcal}}/{\text{mol}},$ phenanthrene has $30.5\,{\text{kcal}}/{\text{mol}}$ and anthracene has $28\,{\text{kcal}}/{\text{mol}}$ resonance energy per ring. Hence, order of stability : Benzene (A) > phenanthrene $(\mathrm{D})>$ naphthalene $(\mathrm{B})>$ anthracene (C)
Thus, option (4) is correct.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Structure, Resonance and Stability of Benzene

question: Number of anti aromatic compound in the following compound is ____.
explanation:
The molecules, which have $4 n \pi$ electrons are anti-aromatic compounds.
(a) has 4nπ–electrons
(b) has $6+2$ (from oxygen atom) $=8 \pi$ electrons
(c) has $6 \pi$-electrons and one $\mathrm{C}$-atom is ${\rm{s}}{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{3}}}$ hybridised hence it is non-aromatic.
(d) is aromatic
(e) has 4π–electrons as available lone pair with $\mathrm{N}$ - atom will not be delocalized in this conjugated system.
(f) has $12 \pi$-electrons.
Hence, (a), (b), (e) and (f) are anti-aromatic.
subject: Chemistry

question: Most reactive compound towards electrophilic substitution is
explanation: During electrophilic substitution, the substituent already present in the benzene ring directs the incoming group either to ortho and para or to meta position and the reaction may be faster or slower as compared to benzene. All groups which activate the ring, i.e. increase the reaction rate are o , p-directing.Activating groups increase the electron density in the benzene nucleus either due to inductive effect (+ I effect) or resonance or both. Therefore, electrophile will attack on benzene nucleus easily.Halogens are exceptions in the sense that they deactivate the ring, but are o,p -directing.Hence, $-\mathrm{OC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}$ is the most activating group while $-\mathrm{Cl}$-\mathrm{COCH}_{3}$ are deactivating groups.

question:
Assertion: Benzene reacts with iodine monochloride in presence of anhyd. $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ to form iodobenzene.


Reason: Iodine monochloride reacts with anhyd $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ to produce $\mathrm{I}^{+}$which attacks the benzene ring.
explanation:
Benzene reacts with iodine monochloride in presence of anhyd. $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ to form iodobenzene. It is an electrophilic substitution reaction. Iodine monochloride reacts with anhyd. $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ to produce $\mathrm{I}^{+}$which acts as electrophile and attacks the benzene ring. Anhyd. $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ acts as a catalyst.
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}+\mathrm{ICl} \rightarrow \mathrm{I}^{+}+\mathrm{AlCl}_{4}^{-} \\& \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{6}+\mathrm{I}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{I}+\mathrm{H}^{+} \\& \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{AlCl}_{4}^{\Theta} \rightarrow \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}+\mathrm{HCl}\end{aligned}$
So, option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

explanation: The monoalkyl derivative first formed readily undergoes further alkylation at a still greater speed to produce polysubstituted products. Also the alkyl halide employed may also undergo an isomeric change. Due to these difficulties alkyl benzene is not generally prepared by Friedel-Crafts alkylation of benzene. Alkyl halides are less reactive than aryl halides but it is not the correct explanation. Thus, option (2) is correct.

Reason: Benzene undergoes electrophilic substitution readily.
explanation: Even though benzene has $3 \pi$ bonds, it is readily undergoes electrophilic substitution reaction. When Friedel Crafts alkylation is carried out with $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}$, the product is isopropyl benzene because the initially formed n-propyl carbocation being less stable first rearranges to the more stable isopropyl carbocation by a 1,2-hydride shift before attacking the benzene ring.  https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953809/preptracker/questions/q_21388_explanation_1764953808968.png  So, option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Phenanthrene is an aromatic compound.


Reason: Phenanthrene has benzenoid structure.
explanation: Phenanthrene, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953807/preptracker/questions/q_21376_explanation_1764953807326.png   is an aromatic compound as it  satisfies Huckel's rule. According to Huckel's rule a compound is aromatic if it has a conjugated ring system with $({\text{4n + 2}})\pi $ electrons, where " ${\text{n}}$ " is a whole number. Phenanthrene, has a total of $14 \pi$ electrons within its conjugated ring system. This fulfills the ${\text{(4n + 2)}}$ rule where ${\text{n = }}$ 3 . Phenanthrene has benzene rings so it has benzoid structure. So, option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: In nitration, $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ is used as nitronium ion producer.


Reason: Benzene has multi center $\pi$ bonding.
explanation:
Benzene has delocalised $3 \pi$-bonds . In the process of nitration, $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ (sulfuric acid) is utilized as the nitronium ion $\left(\mathrm{NO}_{2}^{+}\right)$ producer. This reaction involves the combination of concentrated nitric acid $\left(\mathrm{HNO}_{3}\right)$ with sulfuric acid $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)$, resulting in the formation of nitronium ions and other species. The reaction proceeds as follows:
$\mathrm{HNO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HSO}_{4}^{-}+\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}+\mathrm{NO}_{2}^{+}$
So, option (2) is correct.

question: Correct order of electrophillic substitution reaction is
explanation:
Electron donating group when bonded with benzene ring will activate the ring due to $(+\mathrm{I})$ effect and show better electrophilic substitution reaction, whereas electron withdrawing groups show $(- \mathrm{I})$ effect, will decreases the nature of electrophilic substitution
(1) Here, alkyl group (i.e. $-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ ) show $(+\mathrm{I})$ effect, but lesser than $-\mathrm{OCH}_{3}$ due to presence of oxygen atom, thus $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{OCH}_{3}$ show better electrophilic substitution than that of $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$.
(2) Chlorine, i.e. $\mathrm{Cl}$ show $-\mathrm{I}$ and $+\mathrm{R}$ effect due to presence of lone pair of electrons, while $-\mathrm{CHO}$ group is of $(-\mathrm{R})$ nature. thus, $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{Cl}$ show more electrophilic substitution than that of $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{CHO}$.
Groups showing $(+\mathrm{I})$ and $(+\mathrm{R})$ effect have more electrophilic substitution tendency than those that show either or $(-\mathrm{I})$ and $(+\mathrm{R})$ effect.
Hence, correct order is $\mathrm{B}>\mathrm{A}>\mathrm{C}>\mathrm{D}$ and option (3) is the correct.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Which of the following statements are correct regarding structure of benzene?
I. All the six carbons in benzene are $\mathrm{sp}^{2}-$ hybridised
II. $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$-hybridised orbital of carbon combines with another $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$-hybridised orbital of carbon to give $\mathrm{C}$ - $\mathrm{C}$ sigma bonds.
III. C-H $\sigma-$ bond is obtained by lateral overlapping of $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$-hybridised orbital of $\mathrm{C}$ and 1s-orbital of H-atom.
Choose the correct statements.
explanation:
Statements I and II are correct, while the statement III is incorrect.
It's correct form is as follows:
$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H} \sigma$ bond is obtained by head on overlapping of $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$ hybridised orbital of $\mathrm{C}$ and $1 \mathrm{~s}$ orbital of $\mathrm{H}-\quad$ atom. So, the option (2) is correct.

options.text: I, II and III are correct

question:
In the electrophilic substitution of aryl halides, the incoming electrophile gets attached to the meta position.
Aryl halides are moderately deactivating.
explanation:
In the electrophilic substitution of aryl halides, the incoming electrophile gets attached to the ortho and/or para position. This is because, even though aryl halides shows ${\text{ - I}}$ -effect, the electron density on ortho and para positions is greater than that at meta position due to resonance.
Hence, the incoming electrophile is directed at the ortho and/or para position.
So, the option (2) is correct.

Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

The electrons of the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bond in
1-alkynes are more strongly held by the carbon nucleus. Thus, there is easy removal of hydrogen
as $\mathrm{H}^{+}$. So, $\mathrm{CH} \equiv \mathrm{CH}$ forms $\mathrm{CH} \equiv \mathrm{C}^{\Theta}$ easily.
Which is not possible for $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$.
Acetylene molar mass $(26 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mole})$ is less than air (29g / mole). So, option (2) is correct

$\mathrm{NH}_{3}+\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}^{\oplus} \mathrm{Na}^{\oplus}+\mathrm{NH}_{3}$

question:
The order of decreasing reactivity towards an electrophilic reagent, for the following compounds, is
(i) benzene
(ii) toluene
(iii) chlorobenzene
(iv) phenol
explanation:
The compound which is most reactive towards an electrophilic attack will be electron rich in nature. So, out of benzene, toluene, chlorobenzene and phenol. Phenol is most electron rich compound followed by toluene and then benzene, the chlorobenzene is least electron-rich compound since chlorine is electron-withdrawing group which makes benzene electron-deficient in nature. Hence correct order is
$\mathop {{\text{phenol}}\,\,}\limits_{\left( {{\text{iv}}} \right)} > \,\,\mathop {{\text{toluene}}}\limits_{\left( {{\text{ii}}} \right)} \,\, > \,\,\mathop {{\text{benzene}}}\limits_{\left( {\text{i}} \right)} \,\, > \,\,\mathop {{\text{chlorobenzene}}}\limits_{\left( {{\text{iii}}} \right)} $

This is because the oxygen atom $(\mathrm{O})$ in the nitroso group is more electronegative than the nitrogen atom $(\mathrm{N})$. As a result, the -NO group as a whole tends to withdraw electrons from the benzene ring, making it less reactive towards electrophilic attack. So, option (3) is correct .

question:
Which of the following statement is incorrect regarding nitration of aromatic compounds?
I. The rate of nitration of benzene decreases at 323-333 K.
II. The rate of nitration of toluene is greater than that of benzene.
III. The rate of nitration of chlorobenzene is greater than that of benzene.
IV. Nitration is an electrophilic substitution reaction.
Choose the incorrect statement.
explanation:
Statements I and III are incorrect.
Its correct form is as follows:
I. The rate of nitration of benzene increases at high temperature, i.e., 323-333 K.
III. The rate of nitration of chlorobenzene is lower than that of benzene.
So, the option (2) is correct.

question: In electrophilic aromatic substituion reaction, the nitro group is meta directing because it
explanation: The directive influence of $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ as an electrophile in electrophilic aromatic substitution is due to the $-\mathrm{I}$ effect or electron withdrawing effect of $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$, and can be explained on the basis of the resonating structures obtained by the attack of the group on the benzene ring:  https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953499/preptracker/questions/q_19815_explanation_1764953498636.png  The substituent, i.e., $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ withdraws electrons from O- and P- positions Thus, m-position becomes a point of relatively high electron density and further substitution by electrophile occurs at m-position.

increases electron density at meta position
increases electron density at ortho and para positions
decreases electron density at ortho and para positions.

question: When a hydrocarbon ' ${\mathrm{\mathrm{A}}}$ ' undergoes complete combustion, it requires 11 equivalents of oxygen and produces 4 equivalents of water. What is the molecular formula of ' A '?
explanation:
Balanced equation for combustion is
${\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{y}}+\left(\mathrm{x}+\dfrac{\mathrm{y}}{4}\right) \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{xCO}_{2}+\left(\dfrac{\mathrm{y}}{2}\right) \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}$
Given: Moles of water produced are 4.
So ${\mathrm{\dfrac{y}{2}=4 \Rightarrow y=8}}$
Moles of oxygen used are 11 .
So ${\mathrm{\mathrm{x}+\dfrac{\mathrm{y}}{4}=11 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=9}}$
Hence, the formula becomes ${\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{9} \mathrm{H}_{8}}}$.
${\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{C}_{9} \mathrm{H}_{8}+11 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 9 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: When a hydrocarbon ' ${\mathrm{\mathrm{A}}}$ ' undergoes combustion in the presence of air, it requires 9.5 equivalents of oxygen and produces 3 equivalents of water. What is the molecular formula of A?
explanation:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{H}_{y}+\left(x+\dfrac{y}{4}\right) \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{xCO}_{2}+\dfrac{y}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}$
Given, ${\mathrm{\dfrac{\mathrm{y}}{2}=3 \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=6}}$
${\mathrm{x+\dfrac{6}{4}=9.5 \Rightarrow x=8}}$
Thus, hydrocarbon is ${\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{8} \mathrm{H}_{6}}}$.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{HC} \equiv \mathrm{C}^{-}$is more stable than
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}^{-}$


Reason: $\mathrm{HC} \equiv \mathrm{C}^{-}$has more s-character than
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}^{-}$.
explanation:
$\mathrm{HC} \equiv \mathrm{C}^{-}$has $50 \%$ s-character and
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}^{-}$has $33 \%$ s-character in hybrid orbitals . Stability of carbanions increases with an increase in the s- character at the carbon containing negative charge (carbanion).
So, $\mathrm{HC} \equiv \mathrm{C}^{-}$is more stable than
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}^{-}$. So, option (1) is correct.

Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question: A gaseous mixture containing two hydrocarbons X & Y of volume 44.8 lit (STP) when passed through ammoniacal $\mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ has suffered reduction in volume of about 11.2 lit. If remaining volume is due to $\mathrm{Y}, \mathrm{X}$ is
explanation: Ammoniacal $\mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ reacts with terminal alkynes containing acidic hydrogens and form alkynides. But-2-yne is not terminal alkyne but Propyne is terminal alkyne, reacts with reagent.
subject: Chemistry

question: A compound $(\mathrm{X})$ when passed through dil. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ gives compound (Y), which on reaction with $\mathrm{HI}$ and red phosphorous gives $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}$. The compound $(\mathrm{X})$ is
explanation:
The compound (X) is likely to be alkyne which reacts with water in the presence of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{HgSO}_{4}$ as catalyst to form a carbonyl compound. $\mathrm{HI}$ and red phosphorous can reduce a carbonyl compound to alkane having same number of carbon atoms.
Therefore (Y) is likely to be acetaldehyde, which is the hydration product of ethyne
$\begin{gathered} {\text{HC}} \equiv {\text{CH + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}\xrightarrow[{{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}}]{{{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}}}\left[ {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ = CH - OH}}} \right] \hfill \\ \,{\text{(X)}} \hfill \\ \rightleftharpoons {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ - CHO}} \hfill \\ {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{(Y)}} \hfill \\ {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{CHO + 4HI}}\xrightarrow{{{\text{red}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{P}}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ + 2}}{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} $
Therefore, $(\mathrm{X})$ is ethyne.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Given below are two statements.
Statement I : The presence of weaker $\pi$-bonds make alkenes less stable than alkanes.
Statement II : The strength of the double bond is greater than that of carbon-carbon single bond.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.

options.text: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct.
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect.
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect.
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: Alkynes are more reactive than alkenes towards catalytic hydrogenation

Reason: Alkynes are less reactive towards electrophilic reaction than alkenes
explanation:
Alkynes are more reactive than alkenes towards catalytic hydrogenation because
they are more easily adsorbed on the catalytic surface due to less steric hinderance around the
multiple bonds.
Alkynes are less reactive than alkenes towards electrophilic substitution because the pi electrons
are tightly held in the more compact triple bond as compared to a double bond. Thus, both
assertion and reason are true but reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry
